questions.stem: For a system of $N$ particles with masses $m_i$ at positions $\vec{r}_i$ and total mass $M = \sum_i m_i$, the position of the centre of mass is defined as which of the following?
questions.explanation: The centre of mass is the mass-weighted average of the particle positions, so each position is weighted by its own mass $m_i$ and the sum is divided by the total mass $M$. Dropping the masses inside the sum, as in $\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i \vec{r}_i$, gives the ordinary geometric average of the points and ignores how the mass is distributed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two point masses lie on the $x$-axis: $2\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 10\ \text{m}$. Where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The centre of mass is $X = (m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2)/(m_1 + m_2) = (2\cdot 0 + 3\cdot 10)/(2+3) = 30/5 = 6\ \text{m}$. It sits closer to the heavier $3\ \text{kg}$ mass, as expected. The trap is $5\ \text{m}$, the geometric midpoint, which would only be the centre of mass if the two masses were equal.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Three particles sit in the plane: $1\ \text{kg}$ at $(0,0)$, $2\ \text{kg}$ at $(6,0)$, and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $(0,4)$, with coordinates in metres. What are the coordinates of the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The total mass is $1+2+3 = 6\ \text{kg}$. The $x$-coordinate is $(1\cdot 0 + 2\cdot 6 + 3\cdot 0)/6 = 12/6 = 2\ \text{m}$, and the $y$-coordinate is $(1\cdot 0 + 2\cdot 0 + 3\cdot 4)/6 = 12/6 = 2\ \text{m}$. Each coordinate is computed separately using the same total mass in the denominator. The trap is $(3,2)$, which divides the $x$-sum by the wrong total.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The total linear momentum of a system of particles, $\vec{P} = \sum_i m_i \vec{v}_i$, is related to the centre-of-mass velocity $\vec{V}$ by which expression?
questions.explanation: Differentiating $M\vec{R} = \sum_i m_i \vec{r}_i$ with respect to time gives $M\vec{V} = \sum_i m_i \vec{v}_i = \vec{P}$. So the total momentum of the system equals the total mass times the velocity of the centre of mass, exactly as if all the mass were concentrated at that single point. The other forms either invert the mass or drop the mass weighting from the sum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles move along a straight line: a $2\ \text{kg}$ mass at $3\ \text{m/s}$ in the $+x$ direction and a $3\ \text{kg}$ mass at $1\ \text{m/s}$ in the $-x$ direction. What is the total linear momentum of the system?
questions.explanation: Momentum is a vector, so the signs of the velocities matter: $P = (2)(+3) + (3)(-1) = 6 - 3 = +3\ \text{kg m/s}$, pointing in $+x$. The two contributions partly cancel because the masses move in opposite directions. The trap is $9\ \text{kg m/s}$, which adds the magnitudes $6$ and $3$ as if both masses moved the same way.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Using the data of the previous setup, a $2\ \text{kg}$ mass moving at $+3\ \text{m/s}$ and a $3\ \text{kg}$ mass moving at $-1\ \text{m/s}$, what is the velocity of the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The centre-of-mass velocity is the total momentum divided by the total mass: $V = P/M = (+3)/(2+3) = 0.6\ \text{m/s}$ in $+x$. Because $\vec{P} = M\vec{V}$, once the momentum $+3\ \text{kg m/s}$ is known the division by $5\ \text{kg}$ is immediate. The trap is $1.8\ \text{m/s}$, which divides the wrong momentum, $9$, by the mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Newton's second law for a system of particles reads $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{d\vec{P}}{dt}$. Why do the internal forces between the particles not appear in this equation?
options: Because internal forces are always zero
Because internal forces act only on the centre of mass
Because by Newton's third law internal forces occur in equal and opposite pairs that cancel in the sum
Because internal forces are perpendicular to all velocities
questions.explanation: When the equations of motion of all particles are added, every internal force $\vec{f}_{ij}$ is matched by its Newton's third law partner $\vec{f}_{ji} = -\vec{f}_{ij}$, so the internal forces sum to zero. Only the external forces survive, giving $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = d\vec{P}/dt$. The internal forces are not individually zero; they simply cancel in pairs when the whole system is summed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: An external net force of zero acts on a system of particles. Which quantity is therefore conserved?
options: The position of the centre of mass
The total angular momentum about every axis
questions.explanation: Since $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = d\vec{P}/dt$, a vanishing net external force makes $d\vec{P}/dt = 0$, so the total linear momentum is constant in time. Kinetic energy need not be conserved, because internal forces such as those in an inelastic collision can convert it to heat. The centre of mass still moves at constant velocity rather than staying fixed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A shell of mass $4\ \text{kg}$ moving horizontally at $10\ \text{m/s}$ explodes into two pieces. One piece, of mass $1\ \text{kg}$, flies forward at $30\ \text{m/s}$. What is the velocity of the other $3\ \text{kg}$ piece?
questions.explanation: The explosion is driven by internal forces, so the total momentum is conserved: $4\cdot 10 = 1\cdot 30 + 3\cdot v$, giving $40 = 30 + 3v$, so $v = 10/3 \approx 3.3\ \text{m/s}$ forward. The heavier fragment continues in the original direction, just more slowly. The trap is $13.3\ \text{m/s}$, which forgets the original momentum of the shell and sets $3v = 40$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The total angular momentum of a system of particles about a fixed origin is $\vec{L} = \sum_i \vec{r}_i \times m_i \vec{v}_i$. Its time rate of change equals which quantity?
questions.explanation: Differentiating $\vec{L}$ and using Newton's third law with central internal forces, the internal torques cancel in pairs, leaving $d\vec{L}/dt = \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}}$. So the total external torque alone changes the total angular momentum. A force, rather than a torque, governs the linear momentum, not the angular momentum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A single particle of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ is at position $\vec{r} = (3, 0)\ \text{m}$ and moves with velocity $\vec{v} = (0, 4)\ \text{m/s}$. What is the magnitude of its angular momentum about the origin?
questions.explanation: In two dimensions the angular momentum is $L = m(x v_y - y v_x) = 2(3\cdot 4 - 0\cdot 0) = 24\ \text{kg m}^2/\text{s}$. The position and velocity here are perpendicular, so the cross product takes its maximum value $m r v = 2\cdot 3\cdot 4 = 24$. The trap is $12\ \text{kg m}^2/\text{s}$, which forgets the mass factor and reports only $r v$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Konig's theorem splits the total kinetic energy of a system into two parts. Which decomposition is correct, with $M$ the total mass, $\vec{V}$ the centre-of-mass velocity, and $T'$ the kinetic energy of motion relative to the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: Konig's theorem states that the total kinetic energy is the kinetic energy of the centre of mass, $\tfrac{1}{2}MV^2$, plus the kinetic energy of the particles measured in the centre-of-mass frame, $T'$. The cross term that would appear vanishes because the total momentum relative to the centre of mass is zero. The two pieces add, they do not subtract or multiply.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A system has a $2\ \text{kg}$ particle moving at $4\ \text{m/s}$ and a $3\ \text{kg}$ particle moving at $2\ \text{m/s}$, the speeds measured in the laboratory frame. What is the total kinetic energy of the system?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is additive over particles: $T = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(4)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(2)^2 = 16 + 6 = 22\ \text{J}$. Each term squares the speed before halving and multiplying by the mass. The trap is $44\ \text{J}$, which forgets the factor of one half in both terms.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles, each of mass $m$, are located at $x = -a$ and $x = +a$ and move with velocities $+u$ and $-u$ respectively along the $x$-axis. What is the velocity of the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The centre-of-mass velocity is $V = (m\cdot u + m\cdot(-u))/(2m) = 0$. The two equal masses carry equal and opposite momenta, which cancel exactly, so the centre of mass stays put even though both particles are moving. The trap is $u$, which averages the speeds while ignoring that the velocities point in opposite directions.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two skaters, of mass $50\ \text{kg}$ and $75\ \text{kg}$, stand at rest on frictionless ice and push off each other. The $50\ \text{kg}$ skater moves off at $3\ \text{m/s}$. What is the speed of the $75\ \text{kg}$ skater?
questions.explanation: The total momentum starts at zero and is conserved, since the push is an internal force and the ice is frictionless: $0 = 50(3) - 75 v$, so $v = 150/75 = 2\ \text{m/s}$ in the opposite direction. The lighter skater moves faster, which is why the speeds are unequal. The trap is $3\ \text{m/s}$, which wrongly assumes the two skaters recoil at the same speed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles interact only with each other; no external forces act. What can be said about the acceleration of the centre of mass of the system?
options: It equals the sum of the internal forces divided by the total mass
It is directed toward the heavier particle
It oscillates as the particles attract and repel
It is zero, so the centre of mass moves at constant velocity
questions.explanation: With no external force, $M\vec{A} = \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, so the acceleration of the centre of mass is zero and it travels in a straight line at constant velocity. The internal forces, however complicated, cannot accelerate the centre of mass because they cancel in pairs. The two particles may speed up, slow down, or orbit, but their centre of mass drifts uniformly.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A $1\ \text{kg}$ ball moving at $6\ \text{m/s}$ collides head-on with a stationary $2\ \text{kg}$ ball and the two stick together. What is their common velocity just after the collision?
questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in the collision: $1\cdot 6 + 2\cdot 0 = (1+2)v$, so $v = 6/3 = 2\ \text{m/s}$. The objects stick, so this is a perfectly inelastic collision and the common velocity is simply the centre-of-mass velocity. The trap is $3\ \text{m/s}$, which divides the initial momentum by the wrong total mass of $2\ \text{kg}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: For the perfectly inelastic collision of a $1\ \text{kg}$ ball at $6\ \text{m/s}$ with a stationary $2\ \text{kg}$ ball that stick together at $2\ \text{m/s}$, how much kinetic energy is lost?
questions.explanation: The kinetic energy before is $\tfrac{1}{2}(1)(6)^2 = 18\ \text{J}$, and after is $\tfrac{1}{2}(3)(2)^2 = 6\ \text{J}$, so the loss is $18 - 6 = 12\ \text{J}$. Momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not, because the internal forces that bind the balls do negative work. The trap is $0\ \text{J}$, which wrongly assumes energy is conserved as well as momentum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A uniform rod of length $L$ has a point mass equal to the rod's own mass attached at one end. Measuring from the far (free) end of the rod, where is the centre of mass of the rod-plus-mass system?
questions.explanation: Let the rod have mass $m$ with its own centre of mass at $L/2$ from the free end, and the added point mass $m$ at the end $L$. Then $X = (m\cdot L/2 + m\cdot L)/(2m) = (L/2 + L)/2 = (3L/2)/2 = 3L/4$. The extra mass at the end pulls the combined centre of mass toward that end, past the rod's own midpoint. The trap is $L/2$, the centre of the bare rod, which ignores the attached mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Three particles have momenta $\vec{p}_1 = (2, 0)$, $\vec{p}_2 = (-1, 3)$, and $\vec{p}_3 = (0, -1)$, in $\text{kg m/s}$. What is the magnitude of the total linear momentum of the system?
questions.explanation: Add the momenta component by component: $\vec{P} = (2-1+0,\ 0+3-1) = (1, 2)\ \text{kg m/s}$. Its magnitude is $\sqrt{1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5}\ \text{kg m/s}$. The components must be summed first, then combined by Pythagoras. The trap is $\sqrt{13}$, which forgets one of the contributions when forming the resultant.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A man of mass $60\ \text{kg}$ stands at one end of a stationary boat of mass $120\ \text{kg}$ floating on still water. He walks $3\ \text{m}$ along the boat. Ignoring water resistance, how far does the boat move relative to the water?
questions.explanation: No external horizontal force acts, so the centre of mass stays fixed. If the boat moves $x$ backward, the man moves $3 - x$ forward relative to the water, and $60(3 - x) = 120 x$ gives $180 = 180x$, so $x = 1\ \text{m}$. The heavier boat moves less than the lighter man. The trap is $1.5\ \text{m}$, which treats the masses as equal and splits the $3\ \text{m}$ evenly.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly distinguishes internal and external forces for a system of particles?
options: Internal forces can change the total momentum but external forces cannot
External forces can change the total momentum, while internal forces cancel in pairs and cannot
Both internal and external forces change the total momentum equally
Neither internal nor external forces can change the total momentum
questions.explanation: Internal forces between members of the system occur in Newton's third law pairs and sum to zero, so they cannot change the total momentum, though they can redistribute it and change the kinetic energy. Only external forces, applied from outside the system, can alter the total momentum, through $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = d\vec{P}/dt$. This is exactly why an isolated system conserves momentum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A $5\ \text{kg}$ particle at $(2, 1)\ \text{m}$ and a $3\ \text{kg}$ particle at $(-2, 5)\ \text{m}$ make up a system. Where is its centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The total mass is $8\ \text{kg}$. The $x$-coordinate is $(5\cdot 2 + 3\cdot(-2))/8 = (10 - 6)/8 = 4/8 = 0.5\ \text{m}$, and the $y$-coordinate is $(5\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 5)/8 = (5 + 15)/8 = 20/8 = 2.5\ \text{m}$. The result lies nearer the heavier $5\ \text{kg}$ particle. The trap is $(0,3)$, the plain midpoint of the two points, which ignores the unequal masses.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two point masses lie on the $x$-axis: $4\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$ and $6\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 20\ \text{m}$. Find the position of the centre of mass.
questions.solution: The total mass is $4 + 6 = $ {{1}} kg. The centre of mass is $X = (4\cdot 0 + 6\cdot 20)/M = 120/M = $ {{2}} metres. It lies nearer the {{3}} mass.
questions.explanation: The total mass is $10\ \text{kg}$ and the mass-weighted position is $120/10 = 12\ \text{m}$. Because the $6\ \text{kg}$ mass is heavier, the centre of mass sits closer to it than to the origin.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A $3\ \text{kg}$ particle moves at $+4\ \text{m/s}$ and a $2\ \text{kg}$ particle moves at $-1\ \text{m/s}$ along the $x$-axis. Find the total momentum and the centre-of-mass velocity.
questions.solution: The total momentum is $P = 3(+4) + 2(-1) = 12 - 2 = $ {{1}} kilogram metres per second. Dividing by the total mass $5\ \text{kg}$, the centre-of-mass velocity is $V = $ {{2}} metres per second.
questions.explanation: The momenta partly cancel, leaving $P = +10\ \text{kg m/s}$. Since $\vec{P} = M\vec{V}$, the centre-of-mass velocity is $10/5 = 2\ \text{m/s}$ in $+x$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A system has a $4\ \text{kg}$ particle moving at $3\ \text{m/s}$ and a $3\ \text{kg}$ particle moving at $2\ \text{m/s}$. Find the total kinetic energy of the system.
questions.solution: The first particle contributes $\tfrac{1}{2}(4)(3)^2 = $ {{1}} joules and the second contributes $\tfrac{1}{2}(3)(2)^2 = $ {{2}} joules. The total kinetic energy is their sum, {{3}} joules.
questions.explanation: The terms are $\tfrac{1}{2}(4)(9) = 18\ \text{J}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}(3)(4) = 6\ \text{J}$, so $T = 24\ \text{J}$. Kinetic energy is additive over the particles of the system.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ ball moving at $9\ \text{m/s}$ strikes a stationary $4\ \text{kg}$ ball head-on and the two stick together. Find the common velocity after impact and the kinetic energy lost.
questions.solution: Momentum conservation gives $2(9) = (2+4)v$, so $v = 18/6 = $ {{1}} metres per second. The kinetic energy before is $\tfrac{1}{2}(2)(9)^2 = 81\ \text{J}$ and after is $\tfrac{1}{2}(6)(3)^2 = 27\ \text{J}$, so the loss is {{2}} joules. The quantity conserved in this collision is the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The common velocity is $18/6 = 3\ \text{m/s}$, and the kinetic energy drops from $81\ \text{J}$ to $27\ \text{J}$, a loss of $54\ \text{J}$. Momentum is conserved in the sticking collision, but kinetic energy is not, since the binding forces do negative work.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A particle of mass $3\ \text{kg}$ is at position $\vec{r} = (0, 2)\ \text{m}$ and moves with velocity $\vec{v} = (5, 0)\ \text{m/s}$. Find the magnitude of its angular momentum about the origin.
questions.solution: In the plane, $L = m(x v_y - y v_x) = 3(0\cdot 0 - 2\cdot 5) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared per second. The magnitude of the angular momentum is therefore {{2}} kilogram metres squared per second.
questions.explanation: Here $x v_y - y v_x = 0 - 10 = -10$, so $L = 3(-10) = -30\ \text{kg m}^2/\text{s}$, with the sign indicating clockwise rotation. The magnitude is $30\ \text{kg m}^2/\text{s}$, equal to $m r v$ since $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{v}$ are perpendicular.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: An isolated system of particles experiences no net external force. Identify which quantities are conserved and which equation governs the centre of mass.
questions.solution: With $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, the total {{1}} is conserved because $d\vec{P}/dt = \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = 0$. The acceleration of the centre of mass is then {{2}} (give the number), so it moves at constant velocity. The internal forces cancel because of Newton's {{3}} law.
questions.explanation: A vanishing net external force makes $d\vec{P}/dt = 0$, so the total linear momentum is conserved and the centre of mass has zero acceleration, moving uniformly. The internal forces sum to zero in equal and opposite Newton's third law pairs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)
